On track in direction, early in distance. Your 7-day average HRV of 53 ms is about 2 ms above the 30-day mean of 51.2 ms, and that slow upward drift is exactly how real HRV progress looks. The distance is the honest part: the target is 60 ms, and closing a gap like that typically takes months of consistent sleep and aerobic base rather than weeks, with plenty of noisy dips along the way. So judge yourself on the monthly average only; single mornings — even a 54 ms one like today — are weather, not climate. Focus for the next few weeks: protect a consistent bedtime and keep most riding easy, since those two levers shift HRV more reliably than harder training does. Reassess when the 30-day mean itself crosses the mid-50s — that milestone is what makes the fall target live.

A dietitian or physician can help you set a safe rate of loss.

Sleeping 9.4 hours and still yawning usually points to one of two things. Either you were paying down real fatigue — a stretch of short nights, a heavy patch of training or work, or fighting something off — and one long night didn't clear the whole tab; or the extra time in bed shifted your wake-up into a deeper phase of sleep, which can leave you foggy for hours afterwards. I can't separate the two from data, since your log is manual and sleep duration is the only measurement I have. Two practical moves: hold a consistent wake time for the next few days instead of banking more lie-ins, and if the heavy-eyed feeling continues for a week despite decent nights, mention it to a clinician.

HRV is heart rate variability — the tiny differences in timing between one heartbeat and the next. Counterintuitively, more variability is generally the good sign: when your nervous system is rested and balanced, beat-to-beat timing fluctuates freely, and when you're run down from hard training, poor sleep, illness, or stress, the rhythm gets more metronome-like and HRV drops. That's why trackers use it as a recovery signal. Two honest caveats: it's only meaningful against your own baseline, since normal values vary hugely between people, and single days are noisy — trends over weeks are what count. In your case there's no HRV to look at, because manual logging has no sensor measuring it overnight; your log currently shows sleep, like last night's 6.7 h. Creatine sits in a completely different category from banned or prescription substances — it's legal, sold openly, and one of the most-studied supplements in sports science. It helps you squeeze out extra reps in short, hard efforts and supports muscle gain alongside training; it isn't a stimulant and it isn't a gateway to anything. The standard approach is 3 to 5 g of creatine monohydrate daily, with no loading phase needed. Expect roughly 1 kg of early water weight — that's muscle cells holding water, not fat. Two sensible caveats: pick a third-party-tested product, since supplements are loosely regulated, and clear it with your doctor first if you have any kidney condition. Otherwise, for someone training strength consistently like you are, it's a reasonable and boring choice — in the good sense.
